title: Endoscopy and cohomology growth on unitary groups
speaker: Simon Marshall
affiliation: University of Wisconsin; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Abstract: One of the principles of the endoscopic classification is that if an automorphic representation of a classical group is non-tempered at any place, then it should arise as a transfer from an endoscopic subgroup. One also knows that any representation of a unitary group that contributes to the cohomology of the associated symmetric space outside of middle degree must be non-tempered at infinity. By combining these two ideas, I will derive conjecturally sharp upper bounds for the growth of Betti numbers in congruence towers of complex hyperbolic manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicity-growth problem and Sarnak-Xue benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines multiplicities of a fixed non-tempered representation in the discrete spectrum of principal congruence covers, motivates them through Betti-number and equidistribution applications, and recalls the Sarnak-Xue conjectural bound in terms of the matrix-coefficient integrability exponent p(pi).

proof_stages.label: Main cohomology-growth theorem for U(n-1,1)
proof_stages.summary: Under the endoscopic classification for inner forms of unitary groups and a split-prime level hypothesis, the speaker states an upper bound for L2 Betti numbers below middle degree on congruence covers of U(n-1,1), compares it with the Sarnak-Xue prediction, and discusses expected sharpness and known low-dimensional cases.

proof_stages.label: Arthur-packet expansion of L2 cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Using the assumed endoscopic classification, the L2-cohomology count is written as a sum over square-integrable Arthur parameters and their global packets, with bounded (g,K)-cohomology factors and finite-level fixed-vector dimensions; for an upper bound the character-selected subpacket can be enlarged to the whole packet.

proof_stages.label: Cohomology forces large SL2 factors
proof_stages.summary: A theorem of Bergeron-Nelson-Millson/Moeglin-type as cited by the speaker implies that any global packet contributing to degree d cohomology must have an Arthur parameter containing an irreducible SL2(C) factor of size at least n-d, thereby restricting the possible parameter shapes.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-vector comparison with lower-rank constituents
proof_stages.summary: For a restricted Arthur parameter, the contribution dim_psi K(q) is defined and bounded by a power of q times analogous fixed-vector counts for its constituent lower-rank parameters, turning a unitary-group packet count into smaller U1 and U2-type counts in the worked case.

proof_stages.label: Summing restricted parameters to obtain the exponent
proof_stages.summary: After discarding uniformly bounded cohomology dimensions, the sum over cohomological parameters factors into independent sums over characters and lower-rank automorphic parameters, giving the desired q-exponent in the worked case and explaining in general the nd exponent, hence the stated volume-growth bound after quotienting the center.

proof_stages.label: Local split-prime fixed-vector estimates
proof_stages.summary: The key fixed-vector comparison is proved at split primes because the unitary groups become general linear groups and local Arthur packets are singleton Langlands quotients; the exceptional exponent cases require explicit uniform bounds for GL2 and especially GL3 fixed vectors, with ramified places handled by uniform admissibility.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions beyond split level
proof_stages.summary: To remove the split-level hypothesis or treat other classical groups, one would need to control local Arthur-packet fixed-vector sums via endoscopic character identities, reducing for a suitable component-group element either to an endoscopic group with congruence-subgroup transfer or to harder twisted GL_N trace calculations.

examples.name: Tempered and trivial endpoints of the Sarnak-Xue exponent
examples.note: Tempered representations with p(pi)=2 and the trivial representation with p(pi)=infinity make the conjectural interpolation between full volume growth and near-constant growth concrete.

examples.name: Worked U(3,1), degree-2 parameter psi = chi tensor nu_2 plus mu
examples.note: This special Arthur-parameter shape is used to show concretely how cohomology forces a nontrivial SL2 factor, how a U4 packet count is reduced to U1 and U2 counts, and later how the relevant endoscopic group becomes U2 x U2.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the endoscopic counting argument for U(3,1) in degree 2 with psi=chi tensor nu_2 plus mu, deriving bounds via packet dimensions, q^4 factors, and a final q^9 estimate

examples.name: Theta-lift-shaped expected main parameters
examples.note: In response to a question about where the degree d enters, the family chi tensor nu_{n-d} plus a conjugate-self-dual GL_d cusp form is used to reveal why the exponent nd is the natural expected main contribution.
